text:
Investigating pedestrian suggestions for external features
on fully autonomous vehicles: A virtual reality experiment
Shuchisnigdha Deb a,⇑, Lesley J. Strawderman a, Daniel W. Carruth b
a Department of Industrial & Systems Engineering, Mississippi State University, PO Box 9542, MS 39762, USA
b Center for Advanced Vehicular Systems, Mississippi State University, PO Box 5405, MS 39762, USA
a r t i c l e
i n f o
Article history:
Received 9 May 2018
Received in revised form 24 August 2018
Accepted 28 August 2018
Available online 12 September 2018
Keywords:
Virtual environment
Pedestrian behavior
Receptivity toward automation
Human-automation interaction
Interface evaluation
a b s t r a c t
The aim of this research was to identify potential external features on a fully autonomous
vehicle (FAV) and investigate which features would help pedestrians to understand the
intended behavior of a FAV at a crosswalk, improve their receptivity toward FAVs, and
affect their crossing behavior. In the case of a FAV, technology may be primarily responsible
for control of a vehicle, and therefore, interpersonal communication is not possible. The
researchers wanted to identify potential interface/s on FA

Vs to make pedestrian-FAV inter-
action positive, in which pedestrians receive a clear message about the vehicle’s intended
action. In an experimental study, thirty participants walked across a virtual crosswalk in
front of a FAV. Four visual and four audible features were tested. At the beginning of the
study, the participants responded to a baseline receptivity survey, and at the end, they
replied to a demographic questionnaire and a pedestrian behavior questionnaire, gave rat-
ings for the features, and completed a personal innovativeness scale and an after-study
receptivity survey. Crossing time and waiting time were collected from the simulator data.
The results showed that pedestrians’ receptivity toward FAVs signiﬁcantly increased with
the inclusion of external features. A walking silhouette or ‘braking’ in text were the most
favored visual interfaces, while a verbal message was found to be the preferred audible fea-
ture. Females and people from 30+ age group reacted the most positively to the features.
Those pedestrians who often commit errors or who show aggressive behaviors toward
other road users rated the implementation of FAVs poorly, even with the external features.

On the other hand, pedestrians who intentionally violate trafﬁc rules and those who get
distracted on the road were found to be more cautious in the presence of FAVs and appre-
ciated the inclusion of the external features.
 2018 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
1. Introduction
Once road-users enter the road network, they start a constant exchange of information with the trafﬁc environment and
other road-users around them in order to be ready to respond immediately. Drivers use various formal and, in some cases,
mandatory methods to communicate with other road users, for example, turn signals, brake lights, and emergency lights.
Along with these formal methods, many informal methods are often used to communicate with other road users: eye con-
tact, facial expression, and hand gestures (Charisi et al., 2017; Šucha, 2014).
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.trf.2018.08.016
1369-8478/ 2018 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
⇑Corresponding author.
E-mail address: deb@cavs.msstate.edu (S. Deb).
Transportation Research Part F 59 (2018) 135–149
Contents lists available at ScienceDirect
Transportation Research Part F
journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/trf

In this era of advanced automated technology, automated vehicles at different levels (See Table 1) are expected to
dominate the future transportation system. A fully autonomous vehicle (FAV) is categorized as level 5 vehicle automation
technology that operates independently by means of software and hardware instead of a human driver and executes all
safety-critical driving tasks for an entire trip. With a FAV, other than providing navigation input, human operators and/or
passengers are not expected to take control of the vehicle at any time during the trip. While on the road, these vehicles will
communicate with road users through formal means only; interpersonal interaction will not be possible. Among road users,
this change will be hardest for pedestrians who are among the most vulnerable and who often assure their safety by inter-
acting with vehicle operators while crossing a road using informal modes of communication; for example, eye contact or
hand gestures (Llorca et al., 2011; Mirnig et al., 2017). Interpersonal interaction can offer a sense of road safety that tech-
nology is not currently able to provide. At least in the initial stages of FAV’s implementation, pedestrian

s’ sense of comfort
may be challenged because of trust issues with this new and unproven technology. A survey study revealed that inability to
establish interpersonal communication is one of the major reasons for an increase in perceived risk for the vulnerable road-
users (pedestrians and bicyclists) when interacting with autonomous vehicles (Bikeleauge, 2014). Therefore, it is necessary
to ﬁnd a way for pedestrians to interact with a FAV. Recent research in this area has introduced different modalities of com-
munication: visual intent displays or projections (Deb, Warner, Poudel & Bhandarib, 2016; Clamann, Aubert, & Cummings,
2017; Fridman et al., 2017; Mahadevan et al., 2018), auditory signals (Deb et al., 2016; Mahadevan et al., 2018), and physical
prototypes using a robotic hand (Mahadevan et al., 2018) or humanoid robot (Mirnig et al., 2017). Most of these studies used
picture and/or video-based surveys (Deb et al., 2016; Fridman et al., 2017) or real-world experiments (Clamann et al, 2017;
Mahadevan et al., 2018). However, participants’ exposures in a trafﬁc environment with the threat of being hit by a vehicle
may change their perceptions to a great extent. A real-world co

ntrolled experiment or a picture or video-based survey can-
not create that threat.
This study used virtual reality (VR) to perform an experiment in a simulated trafﬁc environment in which participants
interacted with a FAV, equipped with external features, at a virtual crosswalk. The study was designed to (i) identify
potential interface designs for FAV-pedestrian interaction and (iii) investigate which designs would allow pedestrians
to understand the intended behavior of these vehicles at a crosswalk, improve their receptivity toward FAVs, and inﬂu-
ence their crossing behavior accordingly. Deb, Carruth, Sween, Strawderman, and Garrison (2017) validated the VR plat-
form used in this study for pedestrian safety research. The validation conﬁrmed that pedestrians responded similarly in
the VR trafﬁc environment, as they would do in a real trafﬁc environment. In order to keep participants only focused on
the interface designs, the researchers chose to use FAV in this study, which can be operated by without any human
operator. There was no option for interpersonal interaction with the vehicle, as the vehicle did not have any human
operator.
2. Literature review
2.1. Interfaces for h

uman-computer communication
Communication between computers and humans occurs through an interface designed to transmit a message from a
computer to a human (Kammersgaard, 1988; Lancaster & Warner, 1993). The human, based on his/her recognition and
understanding of the content of the message, validates its authenticity and interprets it as information or a request to per-
form a simple action. In the past, computer-human interfaces were not required in trafﬁc for vehicle-pedestrian communi-
cation due to the presence of a human driver. With the advent of autonomous vehicles, this necessary communication must
occur through designed interfaces. For consistency in the trafﬁc system, interface designs supporting pedestrian safety and
comfort should incorporate existing and familiar trafﬁc components (signs, signals) or communication methods (smiles,
Table 1
SAE proposed levels for vehicle automation. Source: SAE International (2014).
SAE levels for vehicle automation
Description
SAE Level 0
No automation
Human driver performs driving task even when equipped with warning and/or
intervention systems
SAE Level 1
Driver assistance
Human driver performs driving task with the help of one dri

ver assistance system of
either steering or acceleration/deceleration
SAE Level 2
Partial automation
Human driver performs driving task with the help of one or more driver assistance
systems of both steering and acceleration/deceleration
SAE Level 3
Conditional automation
Automated driving system performs mode-speciﬁc driving; however, the human driver
must be ready to take back control to a request to intervene
SAE Level 4
High automation
Automated driving system performs driving tasks, even if a human driver does not need
to take back control to a request to intervene. However, the automated system can
operate only in certain environments and under certain conditions
SAE Level 5
Full automation
Automated system performs all driving tasks, in any environment and under all
conditions that can be conducted by a human driver
136
S. Deb et al. / Transportation Research Part F 59 (2018) 135–149

hand gestures) in a well-organized manner. In the ﬁeld of human-computer interaction, new developments need to be easy
to interpret, easy to use, and easy to learn, especially where hazards are present in the environment (Chen, Haas, & Barnes,
2007; Spool, 1999). Developing a potential human-computer interaction interface that can be used with minimal training
(selecting existing interfaces used in pedestrian crossings rather than creating new ones), keeps task time short (in this case,
time to make decision about crossing), and does not create high cognitive demands presents challenges (Issa & Isaias, 2015).
2.2. Current research on pedestrian-autonomous vehicle interaction
Trafﬁc signs are not standardized internationally and signs in use today may be amended or modiﬁed in the future or new
signs added. Also, pedestrians may move to a new place and share roads without further training or testing and still be
expected to understand the signs and act on them properly (Ben-Bassat & Shinar, 2006; Ng & Chan, 2007). Similarly, when
pedestrians encounter a FAV near a crosswalk for the ﬁrst time, with no past experience with it or its external interfaces, they
will need to be able to und

erstand the messages being transmitted and act on them appropriately. It is important, therefore,
that designers propose and evaluate many potential interface ideas for pedestrian-FAV interactions and consider options for
improving their familiarity, compatibility, and standardization. Many research studies have recently proposed external fea-
tures for FAVs to address the communication problem between pedestrian and FAV.
Lagstrom and Lundgren (2015) used a video-based approach and considered LED strips in different sequences to commu-
nicate the different modes of the vehicle (for example, ‘about to start: LED strips shrinks down toward the center’ or ‘about to
yield: LED strips expands toward the sides’). The results indicated that the pedestrians were able to understand the signals
after only a short training and the interface replaced the informal communication of a human driver, with clearer and more
prompt notiﬁcations. Nevertheless, these features do not provide a message about the vehicle’s intention that is deﬁned and
understood by the general public (without previous training).
In 2016, using an online survey with 182 participants, Deb et al. identiﬁed pedestrians’ expect

ations for external features
on FAVs, considering both visual and audible features, and solicited participants’ suggestions. Most of the respondents pre-
ferred a visual sign, such as a ‘walking pedestrian sign’ or a ‘timer clock’, indicating the vehicle’s intention to stop at a cross-
walk. The respondents also recommended including audible interacting features for distracted and visually impaired
pedestrians. In another survey study, Fridman et al. (2017) tested 30 design interfaces for different states of an autonomous
vehicle using responses from 200 participants. The study recommended using a green ‘walk’ in text with a pedestrian silhou-
ette for safe crossing, while using ‘do not walk’ written in red and an upraised hand to stop pedestrians from crossing. The
use of color alone may cause confusion based on different road-user perspectives. In a survey study collecting driver percep-
tion of external features on a FAV, Zhang et al. (2017) found that drivers of non-autonomous vehicles associated green with
moving vehicles and red with a slowing or stopped vehicle; a forward ﬂowing light with moving car and backward ﬂowing
light with a slowing or stopped car.
A Swedish ﬁrm Semc

on tested their prototype displaying a smile as the external interface (Snyder, 2016). When this car
detects a pedestrian, a smile lights up on the front car display conﬁrming the car’s intention to stop for the pedestrian. Prior
research suggested that a smiling face on a car indicated its friendliness (Landwehr, McGill, & Herrmann, 2011). The
researchers at Semcon also believed that pedestrians would feel comfortable crossing in front of a friendly smiling car. In
another study, Clamann et al. (2017) tested a variety of designs for ‘walk’ and ‘don’t walk’ signs. They concluded that pedes-
trians are more likely to base their road-crossing decisions on legacy behaviors (for example, the gap between them and the
vehicle/s and the vehicle speed) rather than information presented on the external display. However, in this study, a human
passenger was present in the driver seat to take control in case of an adverse situation. Studies using ghost drivers wearing
seat costumes to make them invisible reported that pedestrians hesitated before crossing the road in front of the (appar-
ently) autonomous vehicles (Rothenbücher et al., 2016; Sirkin et al., 2016). Therefore, the presence of a

human driver in
the Clamann et al. (2017) study may have confused the pedestrians regarding whether the controller of the vehicle was
the human or the computer. This is also a signiﬁcant concern with FAVs for other road users to understand who is in the
control of the vehicle.
Using a different approach, the Imperial College of London and the Royal College of Art developed a new technology,
called Blink (Peters, 2017). Blink detects a pedestrians’ existence on the road and acknowledges them by showing a silhou-
ette of a pedestrian using visual displays ﬁtted to the four corners of the autonomous vehicle. If pedestrians want to cross
and they’re not at a crosswalk, they can hold up their hand, and the car will stop (provided it can brake in time) and then
illuminate a green walk signal on the front display to let the pedestrians know that it is safe to cross. If they do not want to
cross, they can wave the vehicle ahead, and it will signal by waving back that it understands and continue on its way. One
criticism of this system would be that it puts the burden of interaction on the pedestrian: it requires they have some knowl-
edge of the system and it depends on their ability to ob

serve vehicles and make hand gestures.
The objectives of this study were to identify potential design features for FAV-pedestrian interaction, investigate pedes-
trian survey responses regarding their choice of the features and receptivity toward FAVs equipped with the features, and to
analyze their crossing behavior in front of a FAV with features, when experienced for the ﬁrst time. To better understand
pedestrians’ perception of external features, the researchers used a validated pedestrian simulator (Deb, Carruth, et al.,
2017) which uses Unity 3D and a HTC Vive headset. The validation results from the Deb, Carruth, et al. (2017) study showed
that the participants’ walking speeds in the simulator matched the average pedestrian crossing rates in the published
literature. In addition, the survey responses revealed that participants experienced a good sense of presence in the virtual
S. Deb et al. / Transportation Research Part F 59 (2018) 135–149
137

environment and rated the simulator with high points for usability and realism. The external features for the study were
selected based on previous research outcomes, their feasibility for development, and pedestrians’ ability to interpret them
even without any prior knowledge or training on the feature designs. With a ﬁrst time exposure to the vehicle and to the
proposed features, researchers wanted to investigate whether a proposed feature carried a clear message to pedestrians
about the vehicle’s intention. In order to reduce the learning curve effect from a controlled experiment, only one trial con-
dition (car approaching from the right and stops at the crosswalk) was used and a short list of features with diversiﬁed
designs were considered. The features were green ‘braking’ in text from Deb et al. (2016), a smiling curve (Snyder, 2016),
an image of a walking silhouette (Clamann et al., 2017; Deb et al., 2016), and audible features, such as music, a beep, and
a verbal message saying ‘safe to cross’ (Deb et al., 2016; Urmson et al., 2015). In many situations, a vehicle may not need
to come to a complete stop to let a pedestrian cross. Therefore, ‘braking’ was chosen over ‘walk’

in text indicating that
the vehicle may slow down or stop based on the necessity of the situation. The researchers wanted to study the effect of
external features for FAVs on pedestrians’ choice for features and their receptivity toward FAVs at the ﬁrst exposures to these
vehicles. The researchers had to ensure that participants had looked for the vehicle and the feature for each of the trials
before they started crossing and instructed the participants that the vehicle would stop only if it recognized them at the
crosswalk. Therefore, proposed features indicated safe crossing conditions only and the car stopped at the crosswalk for each
scenario.
3. Method
An experimental study was designed using a virtual reality environment. Potential external features for FAVs that would
enhance pedestrian-FAV interactions were identiﬁed for this study. There were two modes of data collection: simulator-
collected objective measures for pedestrian position and head rotation and survey-based responses on different subjective
measures. The research was approved by the IRB of Mississippi State University and consent was obtained from each
participant.
3.1. Participants
A total of 30 participants

(17 males: mean age = 30.65, range = 22–47 and 13 females: mean age = 31.62, range = 18–47)
were recruited from the communities surrounding Mississippi State University. This sample size is in line with similar stud-
ies investigating road-user behavior (Nagamatsu et al., 2011; Zito et al., 2015). Many driving simulator studies also used less
than 30 participants in their study (Donmez, Boyle, & Lee, 2006; Itoh, Horikome, & Inagaki, 2013). None of the participants
experienced simulation sickness and data from all 30 participants was used for analyses. The study was designed for U.S.
citizens and all participants were ﬂuent English speakers. The subjects had normal or corrected full-color vision and were
able to walk at a normal pace and gait. The participants reported no hearing problems and only two had previous experience
with virtual reality. The study took about 40 min to complete and participants received $20 in compensation for their time.
3.2. Pedestrian simulator
The pedestrian simulator used in this study was developed by Sween, Deb, Carruth, Waddell, and Furuichi (2016) and
validated by Deb, Carruth, et al. (2017). The setup consisted of a virtual environment (VE), develo

ped using Unity 5 3D soft-
ware, and an HTC Vive VR headset with two Lighthouse sensors to track head movement. For this experiment, the Lighthouse
sensors were positioned facing each other at opposite ends, approximately 8 m apart. The area tracked by the Vive was
approximately 4 by 7 m with a 5.5-meter-long crosswalk and sidewalks on both sides.
3.3. Virtual environments
Three VEs were designed for the study: a familiarization VE, a lobby area, and a test environment. All VEs were imple-
mented in Unity 5. The ﬁrst VE was a general familiarization environment (shown in Fig. 1a), which allowed participants
Fig. 1. Virtual environments: (a) Familiarization VE (b) Lobby area (c) Testing VE.
138
S. Deb et al. / Transportation Research Part F 59 (2018) 135–149

to become familiar with navigation in VR in general as well as to interact with the speciﬁc interface elements that would be
used during the study. The second VE, the lobby area (shown in Fig. 1b), directed participants to the next trial and provided
an opportunity to give instructions to them within the VE. A virtual footstep marker, placed on the ground in the lobby
environment, indicated where the participants should stand. When the participants were standing on the marker, they were
instructed to look at a virtual button to indicate that they were ready to continue.
The third VE was the actual test environment that replicated an urban downtown scene. The experimental trials took
place at a pedestrian crosswalk and focused on a four-way intersection of two-lane streets with two-way stop signs (as
shown in Fig. 1c). Participants started on a sidewalk facing one of the crosswalks with the intersection to their left.
3.4. Experimental design and measures
A randomized complete block design with 4  4 treatment combinations was used to investigate the effect of interface
designs on pedestrian-FAV interaction. The participant was the block and the treatments were visual features (4 le

vels: a
walking silhouette, ‘braking’ in text, a ﬂashing smile, a control = no signal) and audible features (4 levels: a horn sound,
music, a verbal warning saying ‘safe to cross, a control = no signal). The visual features are displayed in Fig. 2. In order to
control all other variables and focus on only pedestrians’ choice of features, there was only one trial condition: a FAV
approached from the farthest lane, perpendicular to the lane with stop signs, and stopped at the intersection. To avoid pos-
sible pedestrian-FAV collision, the vehicle was designed to approach only from the farthest lane and provided participants
with enough time to make their crossing decision. As the proposed features indicated safe crossing conditions only, the FAV
stopped in each of the trials at the crosswalk. No ambient trafﬁc was included in this study to eliminate possible distractions
and to ensure participants focus on and clear visibility of external features.
The researchers were interested to explore pedestrians’ preferences for the feature or feature combinations based on their
crossing behavior. For example, how long it took for the feature to convince them that it was safe to cross and how

comfort-
able they felt or how much they trusted an autonomous vehicle to cross in front of it with a normal pace and gait. Therefore,
dependent variables included objective measures for waiting time before crossing and crossing time, collected from the
headset tracking data. A questionnaire (attached in the Appendix A) was used to collect subjective measures in terms of rat-
ings for different interfaces. Each rating measured the pedestrians’ feeling of safety for a proposed feature or feature com-
bination on the FAV while crossing roads. To understand demographic effects, participants’ age and gender were recorded. To
explore the effect of individuals’ personal opinions toward this new technological proposition (audible and visual interface
on FAVs), personal innovativeness scores were calculated based on a 4-item personal innovativeness scale (adapted from
Agarwal and Prasad (1998). Personal innovativeness is the general measure of the willingness to try something new in
the face of unknowns (Agarwal & Prasad, 1998). This can inﬂuence an individual’s attitude, behavior, and perception regard-
ing a speciﬁc new idea (Chen & Chen, 2011; Lee, Qu, & Kim, 2007). Also, to investigate

any change in general pedestrian
behavior and receptivity of FAVs due to the installation of interfaces, pedestrian behavior scores and receptivity of FAVs
scores were evaluated using a short version (20-item) of the pedestrian behavior questionnaire (PBQ, Deb, Strawderman,
Carruth, et al., 2017) and two scenario-based questionnaires from the pedestrian receptivity questionnaire for FAVs (PRQF,
Fig. 2. Visual design interfaces for FAVs.
S. Deb et al. / Transportation Research Part F 59 (2018) 135–149
139

Deb, Strawderman, DuBien, et al., 2017). The PBQ measured ﬁve behavior scores for violation, error, lapse, aggressive behav-
iors, and positive behaviors. The two questionnaire scenarios from PRQF were designed to determine pedestrians’ ability to
trust FAVs enough to cross in front of them and their perception of the compatibility of FAVs with the existing trafﬁc envi-
ronment. In order to compare pedestrians’ receptivity of FAVs with and without external features, the PRQF was adminis-
tered before and after participants had encountered a FAV crossing the road in this experiment.
3.5. Procedure
The procedure of this study is drawn as a ﬂowchart in Fig. 3. Upon arrival, the participants were asked to read and sign the
consent form as a conﬁrmation of their agreement to participate. Following the consent, they ﬁlled out a 16-item simulation
sickness questionnaire (SSQ, Kennedy, Lane, Berbaum, & Lilienthal, 1993) to report their baseline health status. A score above
5 on the SSQ disqualiﬁed them from participation. No participants experienced simulation sickness in this study and there-
fore, were not disqualiﬁed. At this point, the qualiﬁed participants also responded to the 2-item

scenario-based survey on
their receptivity toward FAVs. The participants were then asked to put on the headset and explore the general-
familiarization VE. After this step, they took off their headset and responded to the SSQ for the second time. Qualiﬁed par-
ticipants (those with scores 5 and below) were selected to perform practice walks in the test environment. The practice
walks included three crossings on the crosswalk that was speciﬁed for the study without any trafﬁc in the environment.
In the experimental study, each of the participants went through sixteen randomly ordered trials (4  4). With the knowl-
edge about FAV’s safer operation, participants may assume that the car will always stop for them and they may immediately
step in to the road without waiting for the vehicle and the features. As the purpose of the study was to evaluate pedestrians’
preference for the features in terms of their travel behavior as well, it was necessary to make sure that they had acknowl-
edged the features before they initiated crossing. Therefore, the participants were told that if the car perceives the partici-
pant at the crosswalk, it will come to a stop and they should cross when the

y felt it was safe to do so. By providing this
information, the researchers motivated participants to look at the vehicle for its intended action (cues for deceleration).
The FAV always came from the right and stopped at the intersection in each of the trials. Participants entered the lobby envi-
ronment after each trial. In this environment, they were able to look around, ask questions, and pressed the ‘Ready’ button to
continue with the next trial. After completion of the sixteen trials, the participants took off the headset and completed the
demographic information survey (age, gender, hearing ability, visibility, and previous experience with VR), the PBQ, and the
interface-rating survey, and again completed the 2-item scenario-based survey to determine after-experience pedestrian
receptivity toward FAVs. With the completion of these surveys, the participants were compensated and the experiment
was terminated.
4. Results
4.1. Inﬂuence of feature and feature combination on participants’ ratings
The data was analyzed using SAS version 9.4 for the survey responses and the simulator-collected objective measures
(crossing time and wait time before entering crosswalk). ANOVA (See Tabl

e 2) with feature ratings showed that the two
Fig. 3. An overview of the study procedure. Note. PI indicates Personal Innovativeness; SSQ indicates Simulation Sickness Questionnaire; PRQF indicates
Pedestrian Receptivity Questionnaire for FAVs.
140
S. Deb et al. / Transportation Research Part F 59 (2018) 135–149

factors, audible and visual features, interacted signiﬁcantly to affect the average rating for interface designs. Main effects for
each of the treatments were also observed. Higher effect size for the main effect of visual features implies that participants’
ratings for feature combination was inﬂuenced more by the inclusion of visual feature/s. In addition, the effect size of visual
feature (g2 = 0.151) is about twice as strong as the effect size for audible feature (g2 = 0.086).
Further analysis using multiple comparisons (a = 0.05) revealed that there was a signiﬁcantly higher scoring for the rat-
ings of the walking silhouette and text features as compared to the other two visual features. The verbal message was rated
signiﬁcantly higher compared to any other audible options. Overall, the FAV with no signal feature (neither visual nor audi-
ble) was the least-favored option of all, with a signiﬁcantly lower rating of 1.97 out of 7. Effects for other feature combina-
tions were not found to be signiﬁcantly different. The multiple comparison results for two features and feature combinations
are presented in Table 3 and 4 respectively.
4.2. Inﬂuence of feature and feature combinat

ion on crossing behavior
ANOVA of the objective measures revealed that the audible features signiﬁcantly affected the crossing time. The effect
size was calculated as to be g2 = 0.03. No other signiﬁcant effects were found for both objective measures: crossing time
and waiting time. ANOVA results are presented in Table 5 and multiple comparison result for audible feature with respect
to crossing time is presented in Table 6.
4.3. Inﬂuence of demographic variables
ANOVA was conducted to explore the inﬂuence of age [age 18–30: 13 people, 31–40: 9 people, and 40+: 8 people)] and
gender [male: 17 and female: 13] on external features with respect to the feature ratings (see Table 7), and a regression anal-
ysis was conducted to identify the inﬂuence of personal innovativeness. The results showed an overall signiﬁcant gender
effect on pedestrian rating for features, although no signiﬁcant interaction between gender and feature/s was found. After
gaining experience with external interfaces on FAVs, female participants were found to be more receptive [M = 5.43,
SD = 1.82] than males [M = 4.81, SD = 1.86] toward FAVs in terms of feature ratings.
On the other hand, age showed a signiﬁcant ma

in effect as well as a signiﬁcant interaction effect with visual features
on pedestrian rating for features. The main effect was found to be stronger than the interaction effect. Post hoc analysis
with the three above-mentioned age groups revealed that the older age groups (31+) found it more necessary to have
an external interface as compared to the younger age group (18–30). Nevertheless, young people (18–30) found the
walking silhouette and the ‘braking’ signs signiﬁcantly more trustworthy than the smile or no-visual feature. Partici-
pants aged 31+ also found the no-visual feature to be the worst condition for encouraging trust toward a FAV. They
(age 31+ years) indicated that as long as there was a visual feature to notify them about the vehicle’s intention, they
would trust them. No interaction effect of age was found with audible features. There was no inﬂuence of personal
innovativeness on the feature ratings. Table 8 shows the multiple comparison results for the interaction effect between
age and visual features.
Effect of demographic factors on the objective measures are presented in Table 9. In the case of the objective measures
from the simulator, gender was found to ha

ve signiﬁcant inﬂuences on both crossing time and waiting time with a small
effect sizes. Males surprisingly required longer waiting time and crossing time than females [waiting time: males
(M = 5.92, SD = 0.086) and females (M = 5.65, SD = 0.035); crossing time: males (M = 3.12, SD = 0.095) and females
(M = 2.94, SD = 0.15)]. Age was also found to be inﬂuential for both crossing time and waiting time. However, unlike with
the survey responses, age did not interact with the audible or visual factors to affect the pedestrians’ intended behavior
in front of a FAV. Further analyses with multiple comparisons showed that all age groups exhibited signiﬁcantly different
mean crossing times [aged 18–30: M = 5.31, SD = 0.91; aged 31–40: M = 5.69, SD = 0.78; aged 40+: M = 5.89, SD = 1.00]. In
the case of the waiting time, people aged 18–40 delayed signiﬁcantly less than the people above 40 years old [aged 18–
30: M = 2.62, SD = 0.77; aged 30–40: M = 2.90, SD = 1.60; aged 40+: M = 5.89, SD = 0.79]. Results from the regression analyses
revealed that personal innovativeness has an inﬂuence on the waiting time [b = 0.17, t(478) = 4.50, p < .0001]: participants
with a higher score on the persona

l innovativeness scale showed a lower waiting time before they started crossing the road
in front of a FAV.

(violation, error, lapse, aggressive behavior, and positive behavior) were considered as independent variables. The results,

negative association with errors and aggressive behaviors.
The results from the regression analyses, presented in Table 11, showed a signiﬁcant inﬂuence of general pedestrian
behaviors on crossing behaviors in front of a FAV. Pedestrians’ errors and aggression positively inﬂuenced their crossing time,
while their violations and lapses had negative inﬂuences on crossing time. For waiting time, violation and positive behavior
had a positive inﬂuence on waiting time, but error and aggression negatively inﬂuenced waiting time.

toward FAVs. Participants were signiﬁcantly more comfortable (t = 2.21, df = 29, p < 0.0349) interacting with the FAVs and
crossing roads in front of them when the FAVs had an external interface installed. Also, the participants felt signiﬁcantly
more positive (t = 4.27, df = 29, p < 0.0002) about accepting these vehicles in their area if the features were included.
5. Discussion
The implementation of VR for conducting research in autonomous vehicle interactions allowed the participants to gain
experience interacting with a driverless vehicle equipped with potential interfaces before they exist on the market. Based
on this study, it appears that participants will respond to trafﬁc in a simulator as they would in the real world; they look
for the vehicle before crossing and wait for the vehicle to stop. The average walking speed for the pedestrians in
front of an autonomous vehicle equipped with external features was 1.54 m/sec; faster than the general walking speed
1.20 m/sec (FHWA, 2009). Being in front of a vehicle without a human driver could be the cause for participants becoming
unsure and thus walking faster. Overall, it can be said that this VR setting works well and can be

a valuable mode for similar
research involving FAVs and road users, especially pedestrians (Deb, Carruth, et al., 2017).
The study was designed to investigate pedestrians’ choice of external features to improve their receptivity toward them
as well as to understand their crossing behavior in front of a FAV with external features. The results revealed many inter-
esting outcome on pedestrians’ rating for different features, their crossing behavior in front of a FAV, inﬂuence of pedestrians’
demographics and general behavior on their rating and crossing behavior, and the overall improvement in receptivity with
the inclusion of external features. The following sections provide detailed discussions on the signiﬁcant outcomes of the
study.
5.1. Effect on feature rating
Several important ﬁndings emerged from the current study. The results conﬁrm the necessity of including external inter-
faces on FAVs in order to ensure convenient pedestrian-FAV interaction. In accordance with some of the previous research in
this area (Clamann et al., 2017; Deb et al, 2016; Fridman et al., 2017), text and walking silhouette were the most favored
visual features as compared to the smiling curve while ver

bal message was the most favored audible feature. There are a
multitude of things that can distract a pedestrian at an intersection (including texting/phone conversation), and an audible
message has a better chance of breaking into that distraction. In addition, a verbal message would be useful for pedestrians
with visual disabilities or in poor visibility conditions (Deb et al., 2016). On the other hand, participants might feel worried

S. Deb et al. / Transportation Research Part F 59 (2018) 135–149

that they would become confused by the beep or the music with all the other sounds that are part of the trafﬁc environment
and thus rated both the ‘animated pedestrian silhouette’ and the ‘ﬂashing ‘braking’ in text’ with higher ratings in the pres-
ence of a verbal message (6). The walking silhouette is a very common feature at pedestrian crossings, and thus provided
the pedestrians with something familiar, something they were comfortable with, as a signal of the vehicle’s intention to let
them cross. The participants also found the ﬂashing text message clear enough to understand the FAV’s intention to stop for
them. However, the smile proved to be a less effective feature; the participants did not get a clear message about the vehi-
cle’s intended action. They were confused as to whether the car was thanking them for not crossing the road or was trying to
convince them to cross.
5.2. Effect on crossing behavior
Clamann et al. (2017) study found that pedestrians still focus on vehicle speed and distance when making crossing deci-
sion. However, researchers of this study found that verbal message provided the most conﬁdence for crossing the road with-
out hesitation in the shortest

time. Contrary to the survey ratings, music produced results similar to verbal messages in the
simulator data. On the other hand, beep confused participants to such an extent that they hesitated to cross the road even
longer than when no feature was present. The beep, which sounds like an alarm, was probably perceived as a warning
instead of the notiﬁcation for safe crossing when coupled with these two visual features. Both audible and visual signals
were presented to the participants at the same time of each trial. Pedestrians were able to recognize the visual signals when
they looked at them and understood them, however, visual signals were acknowledged at the time they were played. This
could be the reason for audible signals having signiﬁcant inﬂuence on the crossing time based on the message carried by
them.
5.3. Demographic effect
It should be noted that the females rated the features with signiﬁcantly higher ratings than the males. In general, females
show lower receptivity toward technology, and this was also found to be true for FAVs and related automated technologies
(Canada & Brusca, 1991; Payre, Cestac, & Delhomme, 2014). However, in this case, the inclusion of the ext

ernal interfaces
may have given female participants a perception of safety for themselves and for their friends and families that raised their
receptivity toward the entire technology.
In general, younger age groups are more receptive to FAVs than are older populations, as would be expected. Young peo-
ple are usually more positive about the long-term impact of technological change on life (Smith, 2014; Veruggio, Operto, &
Bekey, 2016). For the younger generations, the interacting features were likely not necessary for FAV receptivity. On the
other hand, inclusion of external features were necessary for the older population (30+). However, the demographic analyses
for this study showed that the older age groups (30+) were more receptive to FAVs that were equipped with interacting fea-
tures than the younger generations (18–30) were. Nevertheless, the speciﬁc choices of the younger people in favor of the
walking silhouette and the ‘braking’ signs still indicated their desire for a clear message on the FAV display. Participants aged
31 to 40 found visual external features to be necessary on FAVs and preferred the familiar image of a walking silhouette; they
did not trust the smile or

the ‘braking’ in text options. After the experiment, many of those in this age group expressed their
concern about getting confused by a smile or text as ‘Braking’. They found the animated silhouette to be a clear and excellent
signal indicating a safe walking condition. People aged 40+ years were more comfortable when there was a visual feature to
notify them about the FAV’s intention; they also stated that they would feel more certain or secure about the message on the
FAV if it were ﬂashing or animated.
5.4. Pedestrian behavior effect
The inﬂuence of pedestrian behaviors on their rating for designs showed that pedestrians who often commit errors or
those who get aggressive on the road rated the features with lower scores. Errors are usually the result of a lack of knowledge
about trafﬁc rules, and aggressiveness is an individual characteristic. These people took a longer time to cross the road in
front of the stopped FAV and waited less before entering the crosswalk. The presence of FAVs, even with the external fea-
tures, would not beneﬁt those pedestrians who do not have enough knowledge about the trafﬁc system or who always
expect to have the right of the way.
On the other h

and, pedestrians who violate trafﬁc rules intentionally and those who get distracted on the road were found
to be more cautious about making a crossing decision in the presence of FAVs. They delayed before starting to cross and then
crossed hurriedly to avoid being in front of a FAV longer than necessary. Those who violate trafﬁc rules intentionally might
be cautious knowing that FAVs act according to the rules, thus requiring them to follow the rules as well. In the case of dis-
tracted pedestrians, they usually take longer to cross roads (Schabrun, van den Hoorn, Moorcroft, Greenland, & Hodges,
2014). However, the presence of FAVs made them uncomfortable; as an unknown, they could not factor the FAV into their
distracted walking and so laid aside their distraction in order to cross. These positive changes in pedestrians’ general behav-
ior due to the inclusion of FAVs can be mentioned as an enhancement in trafﬁc safety. Pedestrians who frequently follow
positive behaviors were also found to comply with rules for FAVs; they took their time to observe and understand the inter-
acting interfaces before they started crossing.
S. Deb et al. / Transportation Research Part F 59 (2018) 1

Finally, the inclusion of interfaces on the FAVS was tested against FAVs without any interfaces and the results showed
signiﬁcant improvement in pedestrians’ receptivity toward FAVs, both in terms of their crossing decisions as well as with
their perception of FAVs compatibility in the existing trafﬁc environment. This ﬁnding conﬁrms the efﬁcacy of including
external features on FAVs.
5.5. Limitations
The experimental study was designed for a non-signalized intersection and involved a single controlled scenario in which
a FAV came from one side of the road and stopped at the intersection. In order to remove any distraction and make the par-
ticipants concentrate only on the features, no other trafﬁc was added in the virtual environment. The proposed features for
this study indicated safe crossing conditions only and therefore, the vehicle stopped in each of the trials. The scenario did not
consider the termination of the feature so that participants would know when the vehicle would restart. This was the ﬁrst
exposure for the participants to a FAV and FAV with features. Making a complex design with too many varying factors may
cause distractions and take their focus away from or vi

sibility restricted from the features. The addition of more complex
trafﬁc situations with signals, vehicle operator, positive and negative interfaces, and environmental sounds could cause
changes in a pedestrian’s choice of features as well as in their intended behaviors. Future studies should consider a complex
trafﬁc system with presence of an operator and pedestrian signals at the crosswalk along with near miss conditions to
observe pedestrian perception of some of the highly rated features from this study. The start and termination of signals
at an intersection would also be important to investigate in future studies.
6. Conclusion
In recent years, transportation researchers and vehicle manufacturers have become increasingly enthusiastic about com-
mercializing FAVs and entering them into the consumer market. FAVs can potentially enhance trafﬁc safety by reducing
human errors. However, the introduction of FAVs is likely to cause a large change in our way of life. Public approval of FAVs
will not be possible overnight. Researchers should continue to conduct acceptance and interface design studies for identify-
ing road-users’ suggestions to help ensure FAV’s successful implemen

tation in the existing trafﬁc system. Within these stud-
ies, the perspective of all road users must be considered.
The current study allowed pedestrians to experience interaction with a FAV in a simulated environment. For all the study
participants, this was their ﬁrst encounter of any kind with a FAV. The external displays were included on FAV to investigate
which design/s can increase pedestrians’ receptivity in FAVs at crosswalks and thereby inﬂuence their crossing behavior. The
results showed that a familiar sign for pedestrian signals (walking silhouette), a clear text (‘Braking’) and a clear verbal mes-
sage were signiﬁcantly favored by the participants as compared to other proposed features. Their crossing behavior was
mostly inﬂuenced by the audible signals, verbal message and music. Overall, the inclusion features on a FAV, audible or
visual, signiﬁcantly increased pedestrians’ receptivity. Manufacturers and transportation researchers should consider these
results and design the FAVs accordingly. Future studies can investigate the feasibility and preference for the reported fea-
tures from other road-users’ perspectives.

Short version of the PBQ (20 items)
Violations
V1
I cross the street even though the pedestrian light is red.
V2
I cross diagonally to save time.
V3
I cross outside the pedestrian crossing even if there is one (crosswalk) less than 50 m away.
V10
I take passageways forbidden to pedestrians to save time.
Errors
E2
I cross betwee

n vehicles stopped on the roadway in trafﬁc jams.
E7
I cross even if vehicles are coming because I think they will stop for me.
E8
I walk on cycling paths when I could walk on the sidewalk.
E9
I run across the street without looking because I am in a hurry.
Lapses
L5
I realize that I have crossed several streets and intersections without paying attention to trafﬁc.
L6
I forget to look before crossing because I am thinking about something else.
L7
I cross without looking because I am talking with someone.
L8
I forget to look before crossing because I want to join someone on the sidewalk on the other side.
Aggressive behaviors
A1
I get angry with another road user (pedestrian, driver, cyclist, etc.), and I yell at him.
A3
I cross very slowly to annoy a driver.

Positive behaviors
P1
I thank a driver who stops to let me cross.
P3
When I am accompanied by other pedestrians, I walk in single ﬁle on narrow sidewalks so as not to bother the
pedestrians I meet.
P4

Scenario-based question
You are walking home from shopping. On your way, you need to cross multiple crosswalks, both signalized and un-
signalized. As you prepare to cross at an un-signalized crosswalk, you ﬁnd that a driverless vehicle is approaching the cross-
walk (with no one sitting in the driver seat). Based on the scenario, please select the choice that best reﬂects your behavior
for each question.
A.
What will your response at the crosswalk be, with the FAV approaching?
1.
I will not cross the road at the cro

sswalk to avoid crossing in front of the FAV.
2.
I will run across the road even though the driverless vehicle has stopped for me.
3.
I will make sure that the driverless vehicle stops before I start crossing.
4.
I will wait to see if the vehicle decelerates before I start crossing.
5.
I will cross the road with full conﬁdence that the driverless vehicle will stop for me.
B.
As a pedestrian, how will you accept the presence of driverless vehicles in your area?
1.
I will be angry to see driverless vehicles in my area; I think they will cause more problems.
2.
I will feel anxious about the presence of driverless vehicles in my area; I do not trust them.
3.
I will be indifferent to the presence of driverless vehicles in my area; it does not matter to me.
4.
I will have no problem with driverless vehicles in my area; I trust the technology.
5.
I will feel excited to see driverless vehicles in my area; I believe they will make my area safer.
